topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the spine of the worn paperback, the paper feeling thin and inadequate beneath his fingertips. The apartment felt too quiet, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in his chest. He’d spent the last week staring at blank pages, the wellspring of his creativity utterly dry. Then, the discovery hit him—a passage, a distinctive narrative arc, lifted verbatim from his magnum opus, repurposed under a different name by a celebrated, and now suddenly tainted, author. A cold knot tightened in his stomach, pulling at his diaphragm. He paced the length of his living room, his footsteps sharp against the hardwood floor, unable to settle. Every time he tried to focus on his own unfinished manuscript, his gaze kept drifting toward the published work, a physical ache building behind his eyes. He needed to *do* something, anything, to break this suffocating stillness.

[story 2]

"It’s all just… wrong," Clara muttered, pacing the narrow hallway of her studio, her hands twisting the strap of her messenger bag until her knuckles were white. The air in the room felt thick, heavy, pressing down on her lungs. She kept picking at the loose threads on her sweater, an unconscious tic that betrayed the churning anxiety inside her. The realization—that the very architecture of her beloved fictional world had been stolen—left her vibrating with a strange, sharp energy. She slammed a book shut on her desk, the sound echoing unnervingly loud in the empty space. Clara needed movement, needed noise, anything to scour the internal pressure that wouldn't abate. She grabbed her coat, pulling it on with jerky, hurried movements, desperate to escape the confines of the space where the betrayal felt most potent.

[story 3]

Marcus kept tapping his pen against the mahogany desk, a rapid, uneven rhythm that mirrored the quickening beat of his pulse. Outside, the city hummed with indifferent energy, but inside, everything felt coiled, vibrating just beneath the surface. He reread the disputed section again and again, his jaw tight, his eyes darting from the text to the window, then back again. A profound sense of disorientation washed over him; the foundation of his own work felt suddenly unstable. He stood up abruptly, knocking his chair slightly askew, and began to pace the perimeter of his study, his movements jerky and uncoordinated. He needed an outlet, a physical exertion to burn off this internal static, anything to disrupt the oppressive feeling clinging to him like static electricity.
